# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: >-
      Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and
      the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never
      moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
    visual: >-
      [visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and
      dies as the probe turns toward and away from it. A freeze frame before
      the sweep carries the prediction card; after the sweep the probe parks
      and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity.
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - "probe"
      - "candidates"
      - "3  2  0  -2"
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: >-
        Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?
      expectedWrongAnswer: >-
        The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins, and
        nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one more honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs,
      even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer
      loudness. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the
      direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three
      ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. Then one periwinkle candidate
      stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds
      still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win. The walk-home
      question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the
      extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 34

1	# How does a data center decide two songs share your taste?
2	
3	## The score behind the song
4	
5	Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.
6	
7	That is the whole magic trick, and it is smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.
8	
9	So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
10	
11	Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We are going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.
12	
13	## Turn the probe and feel it
14	
15	Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.
16	
17	Now picture one amber probe arrow, that is you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.
18	
19	> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. They are long ugly decimals a machine never has to look at. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the GPU does silently.
20	
21	Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.
22	
23	The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?
24	
25	Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right?
26	
27	Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score cannot be reading sameness of items. It is reading agreement of direction.
28	
29	And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.
30	
31	The number measures which way they point, not what the things are.
32	
33	## Name the machine you just used
34	
35	Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.
36	
37	Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.
38	
39	So say it plainly:
40	
41	**The dot product is a similarity meter.**
42	
43	Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That is the machine. It has a name and a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot sitting between the two arrows to mean multiply-pairwise-and-add.
44	
45	> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes. You still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true at three hundred dimensions as it is at two.
46	
47	And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.
48	
49	## What the meter gets wrong
50	
51	Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.
52	
53	Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?
54	
55	If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back to you without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It is yours.
56	
57	While that settles, one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.
58	
59	That is a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.
60	
61	> Honest edges: the raw dot product rewards length as well as direction. We are shipping that flaw on purpose. We didn't hide it. It's the exact gap the next session opens on.
62	
63	For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
64	
65	Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.
66

file.content:
# How does a data center decide two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That is the whole magic trick, and it is smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We are going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that is you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. They are long ugly decimals a machine never has to look at. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the GPU does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score cannot be reading sameness of items. It is reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way they point, not what the things are.

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That is the machine. It has a name and a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot sitting between the two arrows to mean multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes. You still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true at three hundred dimensions as it is at two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back to you without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It is yours.

While that settles, one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That is a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

> Honest edges: the raw dot product rewards length as well as direction. We are shipping that flaw on purpose. We didn't hide it. It's the exact gap the next session opens on.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

{
 "nbformat": 4,
 "nbformat_minor": 5,
 "metadata": {
  "kernelspec": {
   "display_name": "Python 3",
   "language": "python",
   "name": "python3"
  },
  "language_info": {
   "name": "python",
   "version": "3.x"
  }
 },
 "cells": [
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Your music app just played you a song by a band you have never heard of, and it fit. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
    "\n",
    "We are going to build that comparison ourselves, one line of NumPy at a time. No hidden magic, just multiply and add."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "import numpy as np"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, and the size of the entry is how hard the song leans that way.\n",
    "\n",
    "We'll score one probe arrow (you) against four candidate arrows (songs).\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: call it out loud. Which candidate do you think ends up with the highest score, and can any score go negative?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "probe = np.array([3, 4])\n",
    "\n",
    "candidates = {\n",
    "    \"same_genre\": np.array([6, 8]),\n",
    "    \"related\": np.array([4, 1]),\n",
    "    \"orthogonal\": np.array([4, -3]),\n",
    "    \"opposite\": np.array([-3, -4]),\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
    "    print(name, vec)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Here is the rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: use the rule by hand on `same_genre` and `opposite`. What do you get?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def dot_by_hand(a, b):\n",
    "    total = 0\n",
    "    for ai, bi in zip(a, b):\n",
    "        total += ai * bi\n",
    "    return total\n",
    "\n",
    "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
    "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
    "    print(name, score)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
    "    assert dot_by_hand(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"Manual multiply-and-add matches np.dot exactly.\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`same_genre` wins, no surprise there. But look at `opposite`: -25. The tempting guess was that a score can't go below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing? It just went to the basement.\n",
    "\n",
    "The number measures which way the arrows point, not how similar the songs \"look.\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Now turn the probe instead of picking new candidates. We'll rotate the probe arrow through five angles and score it against one fixed candidate.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: predict whether the candidate arrow moves at all while the probe turns. Predict whether the score climbs, falls, or does both."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def rotate(vec, degrees):\n",
    "    theta = np.deg2rad(degrees)\n",
    "    rotation = np.array([\n",
    "        [np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],\n",
    "        [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)],\n",
    "    ])\n",
    "    return rotation @ vec\n",
    "\n",
    "fixed_candidate = np.array([5, 0])\n",
    "base_probe = np.array([4, 0])\n",
    "\n",
    "for degrees in [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]:\n",
    "    turned_probe = rotate(base_probe, degrees)\n",
    "    score = np.dot(turned_probe, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "    print(f\"probe at {degrees:>3} degrees -> score {score:6.2f}\")\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.array_equal(fixed_candidate, np.array([5, 0]))"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The candidate never moved. The `assert` above just checked it. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score climbed, died, then went negative anyway.\n",
    "\n",
    "So a big score does not mean two songs are the same. It means their directions agree."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Name the machine you just used\n",
    "\n",
    "Point the probe along a candidate and the score climbs to its biggest. Point it across and the score dies to zero. Point it against and the score goes negative. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
    "\n",
    "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
    "\n",
    "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "along = rotate(base_probe, 0)\n",
    "across = rotate(base_probe, 90)\n",
    "against = rotate(base_probe, 180)\n",
    "\n",
    "score_along = np.dot(along, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "score_across = np.dot(across, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "score_against = np.dot(against, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"agree :\", score_along)\n",
    "print(\"ignore:\", score_across)\n",
    "print(\"oppose:\", score_against)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert score_along > 0\n",
    "assert np.isclose(score_across, 0)\n",
    "assert score_against < 0\n",
    "assert score_along > score_across > score_against"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "If the dot product were not a similarity meter, one of those asserts would have blown up. It didn't. Agree is positive, ignore is zero, oppose is negative, in that order, every time.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this next part: stretch one candidate to be five times longer, but do not turn it at all. Predict whether its score against the probe changes."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def unit(vec):\n",
    "    return vec / np.linalg.norm(vec)\n",
    "\n",
    "original = candidates[\"related\"]\n",
    "stretched = original * 5\n",
    "\n",
    "score_original = np.dot(probe, original)\n",
    "score_stretched = np.dot(probe, stretched)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"original score :\", score_original)\n",
    "print(\"stretched score:\", score_stretched)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.allclose(unit(original), unit(stretched))\n",
    "assert score_stretched > score_original"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Same direction, confirmed by the first assert: the unit vectors match exactly. Different score anyway, confirmed by the second assert.\n",
    "\n",
    "That's the flaw shipped on purpose. The meter cannot tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\" A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### For the walk home\n",
    "\n",
    "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
    "\n",
    "Try coding it yourself before next time: write a function that scores two arrows the same way we did here, but stays put when you stretch a candidate longer. Test it the same way we tested the meter, with a candidate and its five-times-longer twin, and an assert that the two scores now come out equal."
   ]
  }
 ]
}

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"

    tracks: [video, page]

file.content:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

{
  "nbformat": 4,
  "nbformat_minor": 5,
  "metadata": {
    "kernelspec": {
      "display_name": "Python 3",
      "language": "python",
      "name": "python3"
    },
    "language_info": {
      "name": "python",
      "version": "3"
    }
  },
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "This notebook builds that comparison from scratch, in NumPy, and proves what it actually measures."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "print(\"numpy ready\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## A taste is an arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([1.0, 3.0])\n",
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_a\": np.array([2.0, 6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_b\": np.array([3.0, -1.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_c\": np.array([-2.0, -6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_d\": np.array([0.0, 2.0]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(name, vec)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Before you run this: the rule for scoring a match is multiply entry by entry, then add. Call it now, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_by_hand(a, b):\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, s in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, s)\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert abs(dot_by_hand(probe, vec) - np.dot(probe, vec)) < 1e-9"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_a comes out on top with the highest score. song_b lands at exactly zero. song_c goes negative. Multiply-and-add can drop below zero without hesitation. Similarity has a basement."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: the candidates never move. Only the probe's direction changes. Predict: do the scores change anyway, even though nothing about the songs changed?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def rotate(v, theta):\n",
        "    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)\n",
        "    return np.array([c * v[0] - s * v[1], s * v[0] + c * v[1]])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_turned = rotate(probe, np.pi / 2)\n",
        "scores_turned = {name: dot_by_hand(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe was:\", probe, \"-> now:\", probe_turned)\n",
        "for name in candidates:\n",
        "    print(name, \"before:\", scores[name], \"after:\", scores_turned[name])\n",
        "\n",
        "assert any(abs(scores[name] - scores_turned[name]) > 1e-6 for name in candidates)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Agreement is not identity\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical\n",
        "different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe . (4x probe) =\", score_scaled)\n",
        "print(\"probe . (different arrow) =\", score_different)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "assert score_scaled > 0\n",
        "assert score_scaled > score_different"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])\n",
        "along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length\n",
        "across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a\n",
        "against = -a                     # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)\n",
        "reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)\n",
        "reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"along:\", reading_along)\n",
        "print(\"across:\", reading_across)\n",
        "print(\"against:\", reading_against)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert reading_along > 0\n",
        "assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9\n",
        "assert reading_against < 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]\n",
        "you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])\n",
        "candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0\n",
        "\n",
        "score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)\n",
        "score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"direction short:\", unit(candidate_short))\n",
        "print(\"direction long:\", unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "print(\"score short:\", score_short)\n",
        "print(\"score long:\", score_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "assert score_long > score_short"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.\n",
        "\n",
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

<cell id="cell-0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?</cell id="cell-0">
<cell id="cell-1"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Your music app just played you a song by a band you have never heard of, and it fit. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

We are going to build that comparison ourselves, one line of NumPy at a time. No hidden magic, just multiply and add.</cell id="cell-1">
<cell id="cell-2">import numpy as np</cell id="cell-2">
<cell id="cell-3"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, and the size of the entry is how hard the song leans that way.

We'll score one probe arrow (you) against four candidate arrows (songs).

Before you run this: call it out loud. Which candidate do you think ends up with the highest score, and can any score go negative?</cell id="cell-3">
<cell id="cell-4">probe = np.array([3, 4])

candidates = {
    "same_genre": np.array([6, 8]),
    "related": np.array([4, 1]),
    "orthogonal": np.array([4, -3]),
    "opposite": np.array([-3, -4]),
}

for name, vec in candidates.items():
    print(name, vec)</cell id="cell-4">
<cell id="cell-5"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Here is the rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.

Before you run this: use the rule by hand on `same_genre` and `opposite`. What do you get?</cell id="cell-5">
<cell id="cell-6">def dot_by_hand(a, b):
    total = 0
    for ai, bi in zip(a, b):
        total += ai * bi
    return total

scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}
for name, score in scores.items():
    print(name, score)</cell id="cell-6">
<cell id="cell-7">for name, vec in candidates.items():
    assert dot_by_hand(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)

print("Manual multiply-and-add matches np.dot exactly.")</cell id="cell-7">
<cell id="cell-8"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>`same_genre` wins, no surprise there. But look at `opposite`: -25. The tempting guess was that a score can't go below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing? It just went to the basement.

The number measures which way the arrows point, not how similar the songs "look."</cell id="cell-8">
<cell id="cell-9"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Now turn the probe instead of picking new candidates. We'll rotate the probe arrow through five angles and score it against one fixed candidate.

Before you run this: predict whether the candidate arrow moves at all while the probe turns. Predict whether the score climbs, falls, or does both.</cell id="cell-9">
<cell id="cell-10">def rotate(vec, degrees):
    theta = np.deg2rad(degrees)
    rotation = np.array([
        [np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],
        [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)],
    ])
    return rotation @ vec

fixed_candidate = np.array([5, 0])
base_probe = np.array([4, 0])

for degrees in [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]:
    turned_probe = rotate(base_probe, degrees)
    score = np.dot(turned_probe, fixed_candidate)
    print(f"probe at {degrees:>3} degrees -> score {score:6.2f}")

assert np.array_equal(fixed_candidate, np.array([5, 0]))</cell id="cell-10">
<cell id="cell-11"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>The candidate never moved. The `assert` above just checked it. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score climbed, died, then went negative anyway.

So a big score does not mean two songs are the same. It means their directions agree.</cell id="cell-11">
<cell id="cell-12"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Name the machine you just used

Point the probe along a candidate and the score climbs to its biggest. Point it across and the score dies to zero. Point it against and the score goes negative. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out.</cell id="cell-12">
<cell id="cell-13">along = rotate(base_probe, 0)
across = rotate(base_probe, 90)
against = rotate(base_probe, 180)

score_along = np.dot(along, fixed_candidate)
score_across = np.dot(across, fixed_candidate)
score_against = np.dot(against, fixed_candidate)

print("agree :", score_along)
print("ignore:", score_across)
print("oppose:", score_against)

assert score_along > 0
assert np.isclose(score_across, 0)
assert score_against < 0
assert score_along > score_across > score_against</cell id="cell-13">
<cell id="cell-14"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>If the dot product were not a similarity meter, one of those asserts would have blown up. It didn't. Agree is positive, ignore is zero, oppose is negative, in that order, every time.

Before you run this next part: stretch one candidate to be five times longer, but do not turn it at all. Predict whether its score against the probe changes.</cell id="cell-14">
<cell id="cell-15">def unit(vec):
    return vec / np.linalg.norm(vec)

original = candidates["related"]
stretched = original * 5

score_original = np.dot(probe, original)
score_stretched = np.dot(probe, stretched)

print("original score :", score_original)
print("stretched score:", score_stretched)

assert np.allclose(unit(original), unit(stretched))
assert score_stretched > score_original</cell id="cell-15">
<cell id="cell-16"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Same direction, confirmed by the first assert: the unit vectors match exactly. Different score anyway, confirmed by the second assert.

That's the flaw shipped on purpose. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.</cell id="cell-16">
<cell id="cell-17"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>### For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try coding it yourself before next time: write a function that scores two arrows the same way we did here, but stays put when you stretch a candidate longer. Test it the same way we tested the meter, with a candidate and its five-times-longer twin, and an assert that the two scores now come out equal.</cell id="cell-17">

cells.source: # How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Your music app just played you a song by a band you have never heard of, and it fit. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

We are going to build that comparison ourselves, one line of NumPy at a time. No hidden magic, just multiply and add.
import numpy as np

A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, and the size of the entry is how hard the song leans that way.

We'll score one probe arrow (you) against four candidate arrows (songs).

Before you run this: call it out loud. Which candidate do you think ends up with the highest score, and can any score go negative?

Here is the rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.

Before you run this: use the rule by hand on `same_genre` and `opposite`. What do you get?

`same_genre` wins, no surprise there. But look at `opposite`: -25. The tempting guess was that a score can't go below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing? It just went to the basement.

The number measures which way the arrows point, not how similar the songs "look."

Now turn the probe instead of picking new candidates. We'll rotate the probe arrow through five angles and score it against one fixed candidate.

Before you run this: predict whether the candidate arrow moves at all while the probe turns. Predict whether the score climbs, falls, or does both.

def rotate(vec, degrees):
    theta = np.deg2rad(degrees)
    rotation = np.array([
        [np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],
        [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)],
    ])
    return rotation @ vec

fixed_candidate = np.array([5, 0])
base_probe = np.array([4, 0])

for degrees in [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]:
    turned_probe = rotate(base_probe, degrees)
    score = np.dot(turned_probe, fixed_candidate)
    print(f"probe at {degrees:>3} degrees -> score {score:6.2f}")

assert np.array_equal(fixed_candidate, np.array([5, 0]))

The candidate never moved. The `assert` above just checked it. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score climbed, died, then went negative anyway.

So a big score does not mean two songs are the same. It means their directions agree.

## Name the machine you just used

Point the probe along a candidate and the score climbs to its biggest. Point it across and the score dies to zero. Point it against and the score goes negative. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out.

along = rotate(base_probe, 0)
across = rotate(base_probe, 90)
against = rotate(base_probe, 180)

score_along = np.dot(along, fixed_candidate)
score_across = np.dot(across, fixed_candidate)
score_against = np.dot(against, fixed_candidate)

print("agree :", score_along)
print("ignore:", score_across)
print("oppose:", score_against)

assert score_along > 0
assert np.isclose(score_across, 0)
assert score_against < 0
assert score_along > score_across > score_against

If the dot product were not a similarity meter, one of those asserts would have blown up. It didn't. Agree is positive, ignore is zero, oppose is negative, in that order, every time.

Before you run this next part: stretch one candidate to be five times longer, but do not turn it at all. Predict whether its score against the probe changes.

def unit(vec):
    return vec / np.linalg.norm(vec)

original = candidates["related"]
stretched = original * 5

score_original = np.dot(probe, original)
score_stretched = np.dot(probe, stretched)

print("original score :", score_original)
print("stretched score:", score_stretched)

assert np.allclose(unit(original), unit(stretched))
assert score_stretched > score_original

Same direction, confirmed by the first assert: the unit vectors match exactly. Different score anyway, confirmed by the second assert.

That's the flaw shipped on purpose. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

### For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try coding it yourself before next time: write a function that scores two arrows the same way we did here, but stays put when you stretch a candidate longer. Test it the same way we tested the meter, with a candidate and its five-times-longer twin, and an assert that the two scores now come out equal.

{
  "nbformat": 4,
  "nbformat_minor": 5,
  "metadata": {
    "kernelspec": {
      "display_name": "Python 3",
      "language": "python",
      "name": "python3"
    },
    "language_info": {
      "name": "python",
      "version": "3"
    }
  },
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "This notebook builds that comparison from scratch, in NumPy, and proves what it actually measures."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "print(\"numpy ready\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## A taste is an arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([1.0, 3.0])\n",
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_a\": np.array([2.0, 6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_b\": np.array([3.0, -1.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_c\": np.array([-2.0, -6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_d\": np.array([0.0, 2.0]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(name, vec)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Before you run this: the rule for scoring a match is multiply entry by entry, then add. Call it now, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_by_hand(a, b):\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, s in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, s)\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert abs(dot_by_hand(probe, vec) - np.dot(probe, vec)) < 1e-9"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_a comes out on top with the highest score. song_b lands at exactly zero. song_c goes negative. Multiply-and-add can drop below zero without hesitation. Similarity has a basement."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: the candidates never move. Only the probe's direction changes. Predict: do the scores change anyway, even though nothing about the songs changed?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def rotate(v, theta):\n",
        "    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)\n",
        "    return np.array([c * v[0] - s * v[1], s * v[0] + c * v[1]])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_turned = rotate(probe, np.pi / 2)\n",
        "scores_turned = {name: dot_by_hand(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe was:\", probe, \"-> now:\", probe_turned)\n",
        "for name in candidates:\n",
        "    print(name, \"before:\", scores[name], \"after:\", scores_turned[name])\n",
        "\n",
        "assert any(abs(scores[name] - scores_turned[name]) > 1e-6 for name in candidates)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Agreement is not identity\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical\n",
        "different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe . (4x probe) =\", score_scaled)\n",
        "print(\"probe . (different arrow) =\", score_different)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "assert score_scaled > 0\n",
        "assert score_scaled > score_different"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])\n",
        "along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length\n",
        "across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a\n",
        "against = -a                     # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)\n",
        "reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)\n",
        "reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"along:\", reading_along)\n",
        "print(\"across:\", reading_across)\n",
        "print(\"against:\", reading_against)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert reading_along > 0\n",
        "assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9\n",
        "assert reading_against < 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]\n",
        "you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])\n",
        "candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0\n",
        "\n",
        "score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)\n",
        "score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"direction short:\", unit(candidate_short))\n",
        "print(\"direction long:\", unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "print(\"score short:\", score_short)\n",
        "print(\"score long:\", score_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "assert score_long > score_short"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.\n",
        "\n",
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Agreement is not identity\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical\n",
        "different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe . (4x probe) =\", score_scaled)\n",
        "print(\"probe . (different arrow) =\", score_different)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "assert score_scaled > 0\n",
        "assert score_scaled > score_different"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])\n",
        "along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length\n",
        "across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a\n",
        "against = -a                     # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)\n",
        "reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)\n",
        "reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"along:\", reading_along)\n",
        "print(\"across:\", reading_across)\n",
        "print(\"against:\", reading_against)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert reading_along > 0\n",
        "assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9\n",
        "assert reading_against < 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]\n",
        "you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])\n",
        "candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0\n",
        "\n",
        "score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)\n",
        "score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"direction short:\", unit(candidate_short))\n",
        "print(\"direction long:\", unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "print(\"score short:\", score_short)\n",
        "print(\"score long:\", score_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "assert score_long > score_short"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.\n",
        "\n",
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

tool_use_result.originalFile:
{
 "nbformat": 4,
 "nbformat_minor": 5,
 "metadata": {
  "kernelspec": {
   "display_name": "Python 3",
   "language": "python",
   "name": "python3"
  },
  "language_info": {
   "name": "python",
   "version": "3.x"
  }
 },
 "cells": [
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Your music app just played you a song by a band you have never heard of, and it fit. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
    "\n",
    "We are going to build that comparison ourselves, one line of NumPy at a time. No hidden magic, just multiply and add."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "import numpy as np"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, and the size of the entry is how hard the song leans that way.\n",
    "\n",
    "We'll score one probe arrow (you) against four candidate arrows (songs).\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: call it out loud. Which candidate do you think ends up with the highest score, and can any score go negative?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "probe = np.array([3, 4])\n",
    "\n",
    "candidates = {\n",
    "    \"same_genre\": np.array([6, 8]),\n",
    "    \"related\": np.array([4, 1]),\n",
    "    \"orthogonal\": np.array([4, -3]),\n",
    "    \"opposite\": np.array([-3, -4]),\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
    "    print(name, vec)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Here is the rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: use the rule by hand on `same_genre` and `opposite`. What do you get?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def dot_by_hand(a, b):\n",
    "    total = 0\n",
    "    for ai, bi in zip(a, b):\n",
    "        total += ai * bi\n",
    "    return total\n",
    "\n",
    "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
    "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
    "    print(name, score)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
    "    assert dot_by_hand(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"Manual multiply-and-add matches np.dot exactly.\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`same_genre` wins, no surprise there. But look at `opposite`: -25. The tempting guess was that a score can't go below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing? It just went to the basement.\n",
    "\n",
    "The number measures which way the arrows point, not how similar the songs \"look.\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Now turn the probe instead of picking new candidates. We'll rotate the probe arrow through five angles and score it against one fixed candidate.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: predict whether the candidate arrow moves at all while the probe turns. Predict whether the score climbs, falls, or does both."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def rotate(vec, degrees):\n",
    "    theta = np.deg2rad(degrees)\n",
    "    rotation = np.array([\n",
    "        [np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],\n",
    "        [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)],\n",
    "    ])\n",
    "    return rotation @ vec\n",
    "\n",
    "fixed_candidate = np.array([5, 0])\n",
    "base_probe = np.array([4, 0])\n",
    "\n",
    "for degrees in [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]:\n",
    "    turned_probe = rotate(base_probe, degrees)\n",
    "    score = np.dot(turned_probe, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "    print(f\"probe at {degrees:>3} degrees -> score {score:6.2f}\")\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.array_equal(fixed_candidate, np.array([5, 0]))"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The candidate never moved. The `assert` above just checked it. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score climbed, died, then went negative anyway.\n",
    "\n",
    "So a big score does not mean two songs are the same. It means their directions agree."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Name the machine you just used\n",
    "\n",
    "Point the probe along a candidate and the score climbs to its biggest. Point it across and the score dies to zero. Point it against and the score goes negative. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
    "\n",
    "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
    "\n",
    "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "along = rotate(base_probe, 0)\n",
    "across = rotate(base_probe, 90)\n",
    "against = rotate(base_probe, 180)\n",
    "\n",
    "score_along = np.dot(along, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "score_across = np.dot(across, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "score_against = np.dot(against, fixed_candidate)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"agree :\", score_along)\n",
    "print(\"ignore:\", score_across)\n",
    "print(\"oppose:\", score_against)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert score_along > 0\n",
    "assert np.isclose(score_across, 0)\n",
    "assert score_against < 0\n",
    "assert score_along > score_across > score_against"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "If the dot product were not a similarity meter, one of those asserts would have blown up. It didn't. Agree is positive, ignore is zero, oppose is negative, in that order, every time.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this next part: stretch one candidate to be five times longer, but do not turn it at all. Predict whether its score against the probe changes."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def unit(vec):\n",
    "    return vec / np.linalg.norm(vec)\n",
    "\n",
    "original = candidates[\"related\"]\n",
    "stretched = original * 5\n",
    "\n",
    "score_original = np.dot(probe, original)\n",
    "score_stretched = np.dot(probe, stretched)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"original score :\", score_original)\n",
    "print(\"stretched score:\", score_stretched)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.allclose(unit(original), unit(stretched))\n",
    "assert score_stretched > score_original"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Same direction, confirmed by the first assert: the unit vectors match exactly. Different score anyway, confirmed by the second assert.\n",
    "\n",
    "That's the flaw shipped on purpose. The meter cannot tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\" A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### For the walk home\n",
    "\n",
    "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
    "\n",
    "Try coding it yourself before next time: write a function that scores two arrows the same way we did here, but stays put when you stretch a candidate longer. Test it the same way we tested the meter, with a candidate and its five-times-longer twin, and an assert that the two scores now come out equal."
   ]
  }
 ]
}
