file.content:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

Before you read on, take a guess: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into a single match score, what would you do to them?

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

      "cell_type": "markdown",

        "\n",
        "Below is one amber probe (that's you) and four candidate songs, A through D. They're just pairs of numbers.\n",

      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},

      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([3.0, 0.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"A\": np.array([3.0, 1.0]),\n",
        "    \"B\": np.array([1.0, 3.0]),\n",
        "    \"C\": np.array([-2.0, 2.0]),\n",
        "    \"D\": np.array([-3.0, -1.0]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",

      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## The rule: multiply entry by entry, then add\n",
        "\n",
        "That's the whole recipe for a match score. No division, no square roots, nothing fancy. Multiply the matched entries, add the products, done.\n",
        "\n",
        "Let's write it by hand first, so there's no doubt what's happening under `np.dot` later."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_by_hand(u, v):\n",
        "    return sum(x * y for x, y in zip(u, v))\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(vec, probe) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, score)\n",
        "\n",
        "# our hand-rolled version and NumPy's built-in must agree\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert dot_by_hand(vec, probe) == np.dot(vec, probe)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A now sits on top, and nothing went negative yet. Hold that thought, it's about to get tested.\n",

        "\n",

    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},

      "cell_type": "code",

        "score_opposite = dot_by_hand(candidates[\"A\"], probe_at(180))\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"facing A:\", score_facing)\n",
        "print(\"turned away from A:\", score_opposite)\n",
        "\n",

        "assert score_opposite < 0\n",

      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same candidate, same length, same everything about the song. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from positive to negative. Similarity has a basement.\n",
        "\n",
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Three clean positions tell the whole story: point along a direction, point across it, point against it.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: what score do you expect at exactly 90 degrees, across?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "reference = np.array([4.0, 0.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "along = np.array([2.0, 0.0])     # same direction\n",
        "across = np.array([0.0, 3.0])    # perpendicular\n",
        "against = np.array([-2.0, 0.0])  # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "agree = np.dot(reference, along)\n",
        "ignore = np.dot(reference, across)\n",
        "oppose = np.dot(reference, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"agree  (along):\", agree)\n",
        "print(\"ignore (across):\", ignore)\n",
        "print(\"oppose (against):\", oppose)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert agree > 0\n",
        "assert ignore == 0\n",
        "assert oppose < 0"
      ]
    },

      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Agree, positive. Across, exactly zero. Oppose, negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle swinging from oppose through ignore to agree.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. The claim below would be false if the meter's sign didn't track direction, so we assert it directly."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# if the dot product were a similarity meter, its sign must track direction alone,\n",

        "assert np.dot(reference, across) == 0\n",

      "cell_type": "markdown",

        "\n",
        "The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# columns: python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free\n",
        "you = np.array([20.0, 15.0, 4.0])\n",
        "role = np.array([10.0, 20.0, 2.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit = dot_by_hand(you, role)\n",

        "\n",
        "assert fit == np.dot(you, role)\n",
        "assert fit > 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "A meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction should hold dead still. Watch what happens to its score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: do you expect the score to change, even though the direction didn't?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "base = candidates[\"A\"]\n",
        "stretched = base * 3.0\n",
        "\n",
        "unit_base = base / np.linalg.norm(base)\n",
        "unit_stretched = stretched / np.linalg.norm(stretched)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"base direction:     \", unit_base)\n",
        "print(\"stretched direction:\", unit_stretched)\n",
        "print(\"base score:     \", np.dot(base, probe))\n",
        "print(\"stretched score:\", np.dot(stretched, probe))\n",
        "\n",
        "# same direction, unchanged\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit_base, unit_stretched)\n",
        "# but the score climbed anyway\n",
        "assert np.dot(stretched, probe) > np.dot(base, probe)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same direction, confirmed by the unit vectors matching. But the score climbed anyway, just because the candidate got longer. A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger\".\n",
        "\n",
        "That gap is the next session's opening."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Rebuild the meter from memory first: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Say it before you scroll back up.\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next, in code: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before the dot product ever runs, then check whether `stretched` and `base` finally score the same against the probe once you do that. You'll have built cosine similarity before anyone hands you the name."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.text:
<cell id="cell-0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
</cell id="cell-0">
<cell id="cell-1"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. No human picked it. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

Hold that question before we reach for any definition: how do you score a match between two lists of numbers? We're going to build the answer with code, not memorize it.</cell id="cell-1">
<cell id="cell-2">import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

you = np.array([4, 1])    # how much you lean toward each quality
song = np.array([3, 2])   # how much the song leans the same way

print("you  :", you)
print("song :", song)</cell id="cell-2">
<cell id="cell-3"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, like "energetic" or "mellow", and the number along it is how hard something leans that way.

Below is one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs). The rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add, one score.

Before you run the next cell: call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score against the probe? Does any score go negative?</cell id="cell-3">
<cell id="cell-4">candidates = {
    "song_bright": np.array([4, 3]),
    "song_mellow": np.array([-3, 4]),
    "song_moody": np.array([-4, -3]),
    "song_offbeat": np.array([1, -1]),
}
probe = np.array([4, 3])

print("probe:", probe)
for name, vec in candidates.items():
    print(f"{name:14s}: {vec}")</cell id="cell-4">
<cell id="cell-5">def dot_score(a, b):
    """Multiply entry by entry, then add. That's the whole rule."""
    total = 0.0
    for x, y in zip(a, b):
        total += x * y
    return total

scores = {name: dot_score(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}

for name, score in sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"{name:14s}: {score:+.1f}")

# cross-check the hand-rolled version against numpy's own dot
for name, vec in candidates.items():
    assert dot_score(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)

winner = max(scores, key=scores.get)
assert winner == "song_bright"
assert any(s < 0 for s in scores.values())</cell id="cell-5">
<cell id="cell-6"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>song_bright wins, because it points the same way as the probe. song_moody goes negative.

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Before you run the next cell: song_bright never moves below. Only the probe turns. Predict whether its score can still swing from high, to zero, to negative.</cell id="cell-6">
<cell id="cell-7">target = candidates["song_bright"]  # this candidate never changes below

for theta in np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 9):
    turning_probe = 5 * np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])
    score = dot_score(turning_probe, target)
    print(f"theta={theta:4.2f} rad   probe={np.round(turning_probe, 2)}   score={score:+.2f}")</cell id="cell-7">
<cell id="cell-8"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from strongly positive to zero to negative anyway. The score isn't reading whether two songs are the same. It's reading whether they agree in direction.

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. It has a symbol now: $a \cdot b$, multiply-pairwise-and-add.</cell id="cell-8">
<cell id="cell-9">target_angle = np.arctan2(target[1], target[0])

probe_along = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle), np.sin(target_angle)])
probe_across = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi / 2), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi / 2)])
probe_against = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi)])

agree = dot_score(probe_along, target)
ignore = dot_score(probe_across, target)
oppose = dot_score(probe_against, target)

print(f"agree  (along)  : {agree:+.2f}")
print(f"ignore (across) : {ignore:+.2f}")
print(f"oppose (against): {oppose:+.2f}")

# the meter reading: positive means agree, ~zero means ignore, negative means oppose
assert agree > 0
assert np.isclose(ignore, 0, atol=1e-9)
assert oppose < 0
assert np.isclose(agree, -oppose)  # opposite direction, mirror reading</cell id="cell-9">
<cell id="cell-10"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter.</cell id="cell-10">
<cell id="cell-11">you_profile = np.array([30, 10, 4])     # python hours, sql reps, nights free
role_wants = np.array([25, 15, 5])      # what the posting asks for
other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights

your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)
other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)

print(f"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}")
print(f"other fit : {other_fit:.1f}")

assert your_fit > other_fit</cell id="cell-11">
<cell id="cell-12"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Same meter, same math, no idea what "python hours" or "nights free" even mean. That's exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.

Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?</cell id="cell-12">
<cell id="cell-13">def unit(v):
    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)

stretched_song = candidates["song_bright"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length

assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates["song_bright"]))

original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates["song_bright"])
stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)

print(f"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}")
print(f"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}")
print(f"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}")
print(f"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}")

assert stretched_score > original_score</cell id="cell-13">
<cell id="cell-14"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger."

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy.</cell id="cell-14">

cells.source: # How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. No human picked it. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

Hold that question before we reach for any definition: how do you score a match between two lists of numbers? We're going to build the answer with code, not memorize it.

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

you = np.array([4, 1])    # how much you lean toward each quality
song = np.array([3, 2])   # how much the song leans the same way

print("you  :", you)
print("song :", song)

A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, like "energetic" or "mellow", and the number along it is how hard something leans that way.

Below is one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs). The rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add, one score.

Before you run the next cell: call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score against the probe? Does any score go negative?

song_bright wins, because it points the same way as the probe. song_moody goes negative.

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Before you run the next cell: song_bright never moves below. Only the probe turns. Predict whether its score can still swing from high, to zero, to negative.

target = candidates["song_bright"]  # this candidate never changes below

for theta in np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 9):
    turning_probe = 5 * np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])
    score = dot_score(turning_probe, target)
    print(f"theta={theta:4.2f} rad   probe={np.round(turning_probe, 2)}   score={score:+.2f}")

The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from strongly positive to zero to negative anyway. The score isn't reading whether two songs are the same. It's reading whether they agree in direction.

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. It has a symbol now: $a \cdot b$, multiply-pairwise-and-add.

target_angle = np.arctan2(target[1], target[0])

probe_along = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle), np.sin(target_angle)])
probe_across = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi / 2), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi / 2)])
probe_against = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi)])

agree = dot_score(probe_along, target)
ignore = dot_score(probe_across, target)
oppose = dot_score(probe_against, target)

print(f"agree  (along)  : {agree:+.2f}")
print(f"ignore (across) : {ignore:+.2f}")
print(f"oppose (against): {oppose:+.2f}")

# the meter reading: positive means agree, ~zero means ignore, negative means oppose
assert agree > 0
assert np.isclose(ignore, 0, atol=1e-9)
assert oppose < 0
assert np.isclose(agree, -oppose)  # opposite direction, mirror reading

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter.

you_profile = np.array([30, 10, 4])     # python hours, sql reps, nights free
role_wants = np.array([25, 15, 5])      # what the posting asks for
other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights

your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)
other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)

print(f"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}")
print(f"other fit : {other_fit:.1f}")

assert your_fit > other_fit

Same meter, same math, no idea what "python hours" or "nights free" even mean. That's exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.

Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?

def unit(v):
    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)

stretched_song = candidates["song_bright"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length

assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates["song_bright"]))

original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates["song_bright"])
stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)

print(f"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}")
print(f"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}")
print(f"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}")
print(f"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}")

assert stretched_score > original_score

A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger."

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy.

        "\n",

        "\n",
        "Rule for the whole notebook: only `numpy` and the standard library. No hidden magic, no plotting library standing in for understanding. If we want a picture, we print numbers with labels."

    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## A taste is an arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "Strip a taste down to its bones: each direction is a quality, and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can hold the whole thing in our head.\n",
        "\n",
        "Below is one amber probe (that's you) and four candidate songs, A through D. They're just pairs of numbers.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: which candidate do you think will score highest against the probe `[3.0, 0.0]`? Do you expect any score to go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([3.0, 0.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"A\": np.array([3.0, 1.0]),\n",
        "    \"B\": np.array([1.0, 3.0]),\n",
        "    \"C\": np.array([-2.0, 2.0]),\n",
        "    \"D\": np.array([-3.0, -1.0]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(name, vec)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## The rule: multiply entry by entry, then add\n",
        "\n",
        "That's the whole recipe for a match score. No division, no square roots, nothing fancy. Multiply the matched entries, add the products, done.\n",
        "\n",
        "Let's write it by hand first, so there's no doubt what's happening under `np.dot` later."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_by_hand(u, v):\n",
        "    return sum(x * y for x, y in zip(u, v))\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(vec, probe) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, score)\n",
        "\n",
        "# our hand-rolled version and NumPy's built-in must agree\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert dot_by_hand(vec, probe) == np.dot(vec, probe)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A comes out on top, and nothing went negative yet. Hold that thought, it's about to get tested.\n",
        "\n",
        "The tempting guess is: the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and no score can dip below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: the candidates never move below. Only the probe turns. Do you expect the scores to change anyway?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def probe_at(angle_deg, length=3.0):\n",
        "    angle = np.radians(angle_deg)\n",
        "    return np.array([length * np.cos(angle), length * np.sin(angle)])\n",
        "\n",
        "for angle in [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]:\n",
        "    p = probe_at(angle)\n",
        "    row = {name: round(dot_by_hand(vec, p), 2) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "    print(f\"angle {angle:>3} deg -> {row}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never changed. Only the probe's direction changed, and every score answered. A big score can't mean \"same song\", because nothing about the songs moved. It means the probe agrees with that direction right now.\n",
        "\n",
        "And yes, scores go negative. Check it."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "score_facing = dot_by_hand(candidates[\"A\"], probe_at(0))\n",
        "score_opposite = dot_by_hand(candidates[\"A\"], probe_at(180))\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"facing A:\", score_facing)\n",
        "print(\"turned away from A:\", score_opposite)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert score_facing > 0\n",
        "assert score_opposite < 0\n",
        "assert score_facing != score_opposite"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same candidate, same length, same everything about the song. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from positive to negative. Similarity has a basement.\n",
        "\n",
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Three clean positions tell the whole story: point along a direction, point across it, point against it.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: what score do you expect at exactly 90 degrees, across?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "reference = np.array([4.0, 0.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "along = np.array([2.0, 0.0])     # same direction\n",
        "across = np.array([0.0, 3.0])    # perpendicular\n",
        "against = np.array([-2.0, 0.0])  # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "agree = np.dot(reference, along)\n",
        "ignore = np.dot(reference, across)\n",
        "oppose = np.dot(reference, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"agree  (along):\", agree)\n",
        "print(\"ignore (across):\", ignore)\n",
        "print(\"oppose (against):\", oppose)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert agree > 0\n",
        "assert ignore == 0\n",
        "assert oppose < 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Agree, positive. Across, exactly zero. Oppose, negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle swinging from oppose through ignore to agree.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. The claim below would be false if the meter's sign didn't track direction alone, so we assert it directly."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# if the dot product were a similarity meter, its sign must track direction alone,\n",
        "# not length. Scale \"along\" way up and it should still read agree.\n",
        "along_stretched = along * 100\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.sign(np.dot(reference, along)) == np.sign(np.dot(reference, along_stretched))\n",
        "assert np.dot(reference, across) == 0\n",
        "assert np.sign(np.dot(reference, against)) == -1\n",
        "print(\"the meter's sign holds: agree stays positive, across stays zero, oppose stays negative\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit.\n",
        "\n",
        "The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# columns: python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free\n",
        "you = np.array([20.0, 15.0, 4.0])\n",
        "role = np.array([10.0, 20.0, 2.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit = dot_by_hand(you, role)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit == np.dot(you, role)\n",
        "assert fit > 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "A meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction should hold dead still. Watch what happens to its score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: do you expect the score to change, even though the direction didn't?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "base = candidates[\"A\"]\n",
        "stretched = base * 3.0\n",
        "\n",
        "unit_base = base / np.linalg.norm(base)\n",
        "unit_stretched = stretched / np.linalg.norm(stretched)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"base direction:     \", unit_base)\n",
        "print(\"stretched direction:\", unit_stretched)\n",
        "print(\"base score:     \", np.dot(base, probe))\n",
        "print(\"stretched score:\", np.dot(stretched, probe))\n",
        "\n",
        "# same direction, unchanged\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit_base, unit_stretched)\n",
        "# but the score climbed anyway\n",
        "assert np.dot(stretched, probe) > np.dot(base, probe)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same direction, confirmed by the unit vectors matching. But the score climbed anyway, just because the candidate got longer. A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger\".\n",
        "\n",
        "That gap is the next session's opening."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Rebuild the meter from memory first: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Say it before you scroll back up.\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next, in code: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before the dot product ever runs, then check whether `stretched` and `base` finally score the same against the probe once you do that. You'll have built cosine similarity before anyone hands you the name."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# if the dot product were a similarity meter, its sign must track direction alone,\n",
        "# not length. Scale \"along\" way up and it should still read agree.\n",
        "along_stretched = along * 100\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.sign(np.dot(reference, along)) == np.sign(np.dot(reference, along_stretched))\n",
        "assert np.dot(reference, across) == 0\n",
        "assert np.sign(np.dot(reference, against)) == -1\n",
        "print(\"the meter's sign holds: agree stays positive, across stays zero, oppose stays negative\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit.\n",
        "\n",
        "The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# columns: python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free\n",
        "you = np.array([20.0, 15.0, 4.0])\n",
        "role = np.array([10.0, 20.0, 2.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit = dot_by_hand(you, role)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit == np.dot(you, role)\n",
        "assert fit > 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "A meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction should hold dead still. Watch what happens to its score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: do you expect the score to change, even though the direction didn't?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "base = candidates[\"A\"]\n",
        "stretched = base * 3.0\n",
        "\n",
        "unit_base = base / np.linalg.norm(base)\n",
        "unit_stretched = stretched / np.linalg.norm(stretched)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"base direction:     \", unit_base)\n",
        "print(\"stretched direction:\", unit_stretched)\n",
        "print(\"base score:     \", np.dot(base, probe))\n",
        "print(\"stretched score:\", np.dot(stretched, probe))\n",
        "\n",
        "# same direction, unchanged\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit_base, unit_stretched)\n",
        "# but the score climbed anyway\n",
        "assert np.dot(stretched, probe) > np.dot(base, probe)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same direction, confirmed by the unit vectors matching. But the score climbed anyway, just because the candidate got longer. A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger\".\n",
        "\n",
        "That gap is the next session's opening."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Rebuild the meter from memory first: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Say it before you scroll back up.\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next, in code: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before the dot product ever runs, then check whether `stretched` and `base` finally score the same against the probe once you do that. You'll have built cosine similarity before anyone hands you the name."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

{
  "nbformat": 4,
  "nbformat_minor": 5,
  "metadata": {
    "kernelspec": {
      "display_name": "Python 3",
      "language": "python",
      "name": "python3"
    },
    "language_info": {
      "name": "python",
      "pygments_lexer": "ipython3"
    }
  },
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. No human picked it. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.\n",
        "\n",
        "Hold that question before we reach for any definition: how do you score a match between two lists of numbers? We're going to build the answer with code, not memorize it."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "\n",
        "you = np.array([4, 1])    # how much you lean toward each quality\n",
        "song = np.array([3, 2])   # how much the song leans the same way\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"you  :\", you)\n",
        "print(\"song :\", song)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, like \"energetic\" or \"mellow\", and the number along it is how hard something leans that way.\n",
        "\n",
        "Below is one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs). The rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add, one score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score against the probe? Does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_bright\": np.array([4, 3]),\n",
        "    \"song_mellow\": np.array([-3, 4]),\n",
        "    \"song_moody\": np.array([-4, -3]),\n",
        "    \"song_offbeat\": np.array([1, -1]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "probe = np.array([4, 3])\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {vec}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_score(a, b):\n",
        "    \"\"\"Multiply entry by entry, then add. That's the whole rule.\"\"\"\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_score(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, score in sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True):\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {score:+.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# cross-check the hand-rolled version against numpy's own dot\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert dot_score(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)\n",
        "\n",
        "winner = max(scores, key=scores.get)\n",
        "assert winner == \"song_bright\"\n",
        "assert any(s < 0 for s in scores.values())"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_bright wins, because it points the same way as the probe. song_moody goes negative.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: song_bright never moves below. Only the probe turns. Predict whether its score can still swing from high, to zero, to negative."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "target = candidates[\"song_bright\"]  # this candidate never changes below\n",
        "\n",
        "for theta in np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 9):\n",
        "    turning_probe = 5 * np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])\n",
        "    score = dot_score(turning_probe, target)\n",
        "    print(f\"theta={theta:4.2f} rad   probe={np.round(turning_probe, 2)}   score={score:+.2f}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from strongly positive to zero to negative anyway. The score isn't reading whether two songs are the same. It's reading whether they agree in direction.\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. It has a symbol now: $a \\cdot b$, multiply-pairwise-and-add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "target_angle = np.arctan2(target[1], target[0])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_along = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle), np.sin(target_angle)])\n",
        "probe_across = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi / 2), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi / 2)])\n",
        "probe_against = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi)])\n",
        "\n",
        "agree = dot_score(probe_along, target)\n",
        "ignore = dot_score(probe_across, target)\n",
        "oppose = dot_score(probe_against, target)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"agree  (along)  : {agree:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"ignore (across) : {ignore:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"oppose (against): {oppose:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# the meter reading: positive means agree, ~zero means ignore, negative means oppose\n",
        "assert agree > 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(ignore, 0, atol=1e-9)\n",
        "assert oppose < 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(agree, -oppose)  # opposite direction, mirror reading"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?\n",
        "\n",
        "While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "you_profile = np.array([30, 10, 4])     # python hours, sql reps, nights free\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25, 15, 5])      # what the posting asks for\n",
        "other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights\n",
        "\n",
        "your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)\n",
        "other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"other fit : {other_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert your_fit > other_fit"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same meter, same math, no idea what \"python hours\" or \"nights free\" even mean. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "stretched_song = candidates[\"song_bright\"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates[\"song_bright\"]))\n",
        "\n",
        "original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates[\"song_bright\"])\n",
        "stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert stretched_score > original_score"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\"\n",
        "\n",
        "For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.content:
1	# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
2	# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
3	# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
4	# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
5	# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
6	# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
7	schemaVersion: 2
8	session: dotprod
9	aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
10	openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
11	closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
12	  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
13	  turn them into the score?
14	breadcrumbQuestion: >-
15	  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
16	extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
17	notationSchedule:
18	  - symbol: a . b
19	    referent: the multiply-and-add score
20	    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
21	    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
22	beats:
23	  - id: beat-01
24	    title: The score behind the song
25	    narration: >-
26	      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
27	      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
28	      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
29	      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
30	      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
31	      learning.
32	    visual: >-
33	      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
34	      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
35	      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
36	      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
37	      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
38	    tracks: [video, page]
39	    newIdea: >-
40	      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
41	      comparison runs recommendation.
42	    novelElements:
43	      - the two number lists
44	      - the match score readout
45	    onScreenText:
46	      - "How do you score a match?"
47	    colors:
48	      lists: data.observed
49	      score: data.params
50	      background: bg
51	      labels: text
52	    estimatedSeconds: 26
53	  - id: beat-02
54	    title: Turn the probe and feel it

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: "Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality,
      and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved
      at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness."
    visual: "[visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and dies as
      the probe turns toward and away from it. Each candidate label sits nudged
      outward past its arrow tip so the sweeping amber probe never buries it,
      staying readable at every probe angle including when the probe passes
      candidate B. A freeze frame before the sweep carries the prediction card;
      after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on
      the bars."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: "Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity."
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - probe
      - candidates
      - 3  2  0  -2
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: "Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?"
      expectedWrongAnswer: The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins,
        and nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    majorReveal: false
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score
      yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries,
      add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The
      multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is
      exactly why it travels. Now the honest look, and guess before you
      watch. If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction,
      longer arrow, does its score change? It climbs. The direction never
      moved, and the score climbed anyway. Long songs would win on sheer
      loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length. For the walk
      home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets
      the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. The song columns swap for two
      labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free;
      matched entries multiply and pool into the same lilac readout on the
      same dial. A guess card holds with one candidate outlined and the
      score readout hidden. Then that periwinkle candidate stretches longer;
      its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose
      flag marks the unearned win. The walk-home question lands as the
      closing card,
      and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the job-fit columns
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "different aisle, same meter"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: "If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer
        arrow, does its score change?"
      expectedWrongAnswer: "No: the direction is the same, so the similarity
        score stays the same."

file.content:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

Before you read on, take a guess: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into a single match score, what would you do to them?

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

    "import numpy as np\n",
    "\n",
    "np.set_printoptions(precision=3, suppress=True)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## A taste is an arrow\n",
    "\n",
    "Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way.\n",

   ]
  },

   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "probe = np.array([4, 3])\n",
    "\n",
    "candidates = {\n",
    "    \"aligned\": np.array([8, 6]),\n",
    "    \"orthogonal\": np.array([-3, 4]),\n",
    "    \"opposite\": np.array([-4, -3]),\n",
    "    \"partial\": np.array([3, -1]),\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
    "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
    "    print(f\"{name:10s} {vec}\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Build the score by hand first\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: write out on paper what `probe` dotted with `aligned` should be, entry by entry, then add. We'll build the score two ways: a plain Python loop, and NumPy's own `np.dot`. They should agree exactly."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def manual_dot(a, b):\n",
    "    total = 0\n",
    "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
    "        total += x * y\n",
    "    return total\n",
    "\n",
    "manual_score = manual_dot(probe, candidates[\"aligned\"])\n",
    "numpy_score = np.dot(probe, candidates[\"aligned\"])\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"manual multiply-and-add:\", manual_score)\n",
    "print(\"np.dot:                 \", numpy_score)\n",
    "assert manual_score == numpy_score"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Before you drag anything, call it\n",
    "\n",
    "Call it: which candidate ends up on top against `probe = [4, 3]`? And does any score go negative?\n",
    "\n",
    "The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score can dip below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "scores = {name: np.dot(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
    "\n",
    "for name, s in scores.items():\n",
    "    print(f\"{name:10s} score = {s}\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`aligned` wins, and yes, `opposite` went negative. Similarity has a basement. That kills the expected wrong answer: agreement is not the same as \"nothing below zero.\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "assert scores[\"opposite\"] < 0\n",
    "assert scores[\"aligned\"] == max(scores.values())"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
    "\n",
    "Now turn the probe itself instead of the candidates. Point it along a direction, then across, then away, and watch the score answer, while the candidate arrays never change at all.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: predict whether the `aligned` candidate's array itself will be any different after we turn the probe three times."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "aligned = candidates[\"aligned\"]\n",
    "before = aligned.copy()\n",
    "\n",
    "probe_along = np.array([1, 0])\n",
    "probe_across = np.array([0, 1])\n",
    "probe_against = np.array([-1, 0])\n",
    "\n",
    "score_along = np.dot(probe_along, aligned)\n",
    "score_across = np.dot(probe_across, aligned)\n",
    "score_against = np.dot(probe_against, aligned)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"score with probe along:  \", score_along)\n",
    "print(\"score with probe across: \", score_across)\n",
    "print(\"score with probe against:\", score_against)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.array_equal(aligned, before)\n",
    "assert score_along != score_across != score_against"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction moved, and the score moved with it. So a big score can't be reading sameness of the items. It's reading agreement of direction."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Name the machine you just used\n",
    "\n",
    "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
    "\n",
    "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
    "\n",
    "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out, written `a . b`."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Before you run this: predict the sign of each score below. One should read positive (agree), one should read exactly zero (ignore), one should read negative (oppose)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "agree_score = np.dot(probe, candidates[\"aligned\"])\n",
    "ignore_score = np.dot(probe, candidates[\"orthogonal\"])\n",
    "oppose_score = np.dot(probe, candidates[\"opposite\"])\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"agree: \", agree_score)\n",
    "print(\"ignore:\", ignore_score)\n",
    "print(\"oppose:\", oppose_score)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert agree_score > 0\n",
    "assert ignore_score == 0\n",
    "assert oppose_score < 0"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The meter reads exactly three stories: agree, ignore, oppose. If the claim above were false, one of those three asserts would have raised. It didn't."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
    "\n",
    "Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Multiply pairwise, then add. That's it, and it's yours now.\n",
    "\n",
    "While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "you = np.array([8, 5, 3])   # python hours/week, sql reps, nights free\n",
    "role = np.array([6, 4, 0])  # what the posting wants of each\n",
    "\n",
    "fit = np.dot(you, role)\n",
    "print(\"job-fit score:\", fit)\n",
    "assert fit == manual_dot(you, role)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Before you watch: does size cheat the meter?\n",
    "\n",
    "Now the honest look. Guess before you watch: if your numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer arrow, does your job-fit score change?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def unit(v):\n",
    "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
    "\n",
    "you_padded = you * 3  # same direction, tripled length: a padded resume\n",
    "\n",
    "fit_padded = np.dot(you_padded, role)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"original fit:\", fit)\n",
    "print(\"padded fit:  \", fit_padded)\n",
    "print(\"direction unchanged?\", np.allclose(unit(you), unit(you_padded)))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert np.allclose(unit(you), unit(you_padded))\n",
    "assert fit_padded > fit"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The direction never moved, and the score climbed anyway. A long, loud song wins on sheer size. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and it never asked how big they were either. That's exactly why it travels, and exactly where it lies to you."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## For the walk home\n",
    "\n",
    "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
    "\n",
    "If you want to code toward an answer next: write a function that divides a dot product by the lengths of both arrows involved, run it on `you` and `you_padded` against `role`, and see whether the padded score still wins."
   ]
  }
 ]
}
