<cell id="cell-0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
</cell id="cell-0">
<cell id="cell-1"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you never heard of. Somewhere, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.

A song is not one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at once. Your taste is that same tangle. So the real question: how do you boil a match between two many-featured things down to a single number?

We're going to build one answer with code, cell by cell, and check it against itself as we go.</cell id="cell-1">
<cell id="cell-2">import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def dot(a, b):
    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    return np.sum(a * b)
</cell id="cell-2">
<cell id="cell-3"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Turn the probe and feel it

Think of a taste as an arrow. Each direction is a quality, and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Here's one amber probe (that's you) and four candidate songs, all as 2D arrows.

The rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add. That sum is the score.

Before you run this: the probe points straight along the x-axis. Which candidate do you think will score highest, and can any score go negative?</cell id="cell-3">
<cell id="cell-4">probe = np.array([1.0, 0.0])

c1 = np.array([3.0, 0.0])
c2 = np.array([0.0, 3.0])
c3 = np.array([-3.0, 0.0])
c4 = np.array([2.0, 2.0])

candidates = {"c1": c1, "c2": c2, "c3": c3, "c4": c4}

scores = {name: dot(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}
for name, score in scores.items():
    print(name, "->", score)
</cell id="cell-4">
<cell id="cell-5"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>c1 tops out because the probe points its way, not because c1 is the longest arrow (c1, c2, and c3 are all the same length). And c3 already went negative.

Now turn the probe. Nothing about the candidates changes. Before you run this: predict which candidate takes over the top score once the probe points straight up instead.</cell id="cell-5">
<cell id="cell-6">probe_turned = np.array([0.0, 1.0])

scores_turned = {name: dot(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}
for name, score in scores_turned.items():
    print(name, "->", score)

assert scores_turned["c2"] > scores_turned["c1"]
assert scores["c1"] > scores["c2"]
</cell id="cell-6">
<cell id="cell-7"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>The candidates never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the ranking flipped completely. So the score is not reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how well two directions agree.

Now the trap. Before you run this: stretch c1 to three times its length, but keep it pointing the exact same way. Does its score against the original probe change?</cell id="cell-7">
<cell id="cell-8">def cosine(a, b):
    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    return dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

c1_stretched = c1 * 3.0

score_before = dot(probe, c1)
score_after = dot(probe, c1_stretched)
print("score before stretch:", score_before)
print("score after stretch: ", score_after)

same_direction = np.isclose(cosine(probe, c1), cosine(probe, c1_stretched))
print("direction unchanged:", same_direction)

assert same_direction
assert score_after > score_before
</cell id="cell-8">
<cell id="cell-9"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>c1 never turned. Its direction, measured by cosine, is identical before and after. But its score climbed anyway, purely because it got longer. Length cheats the meter. A big score does not mean "same song," it means "points the same way," and a long arrow can fake a high number.</cell id="cell-9">
<cell id="cell-10"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Name the machine you just used

Three probe positions, three readings. Point along a candidate, point across it, point against it. Before you run this: predict the sign of each score.</cell id="cell-10">
<cell id="cell-11">along = c1      # same direction as the probe
across = c2     # perpendicular to the probe
against = c3    # opposite direction from the probe

score_along = dot(probe, along)
score_across = dot(probe, across)
score_against = dot(probe, against)

print("along:  ", score_along)
print("across: ", score_across)
print("against:", score_against)

assert score_along > 0
assert score_across == 0
assert score_against < 0
</cell id="cell-11">
<cell id="cell-12"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three readings.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in, one honest reading of alignment comes out. If that claim were false, the assert below would fail: aligned arrows would not score highest, perpendicular arrows would not land on exactly zero, and opposed arrows would not go negative.</cell id="cell-12">
<cell id="cell-13">readings = [score_along, score_across, score_against]
print("meter readings, agree -> ignore -> oppose:", readings)

assert readings[0] > readings[1] > readings[2]
assert readings[1] == 0.0
</cell id="cell-13">
<cell id="cell-14"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Where the meter travels, and where it breaks

Same meter, different aisle. Swap songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what a role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply matched entries, add them up. The dot product never asked what the numbers meant.</cell id="cell-14">
<cell id="cell-15"># features: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]
you = np.array([8.0, 5.0, 2.0])
role = np.array([6.0, 4.0, 1.0])

fit_score = dot(you, role)
print("job fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == dot(role, you)
</cell id="cell-15">
<cell id="cell-16"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Same function, new domain, no code changed. That's exactly why the meter travels.

Now the honest look. Before you run this: if you double the role's requirements exactly, same direction, twice the numbers, does the fit score double too?</cell id="cell-16">
<cell id="cell-17">role_doubled = role * 2.0

fit_score_doubled = dot(you, role_doubled)
print("original fit score:", fit_score)
print("doubled-role score:", fit_score_doubled)

assert np.isclose(fit_score_doubled, 2.0 * fit_score)
</cell id="cell-17">
<cell id="cell-18"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>It doubles exactly. The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling is the crack: a long, loud song can win on sheer size, and a padded resume can win on sheer length, because size passes straight through the meter untouched.</cell id="cell-18">
<cell id="cell-19"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

One thread to pull: try normalizing a vector by its own length (dividing by `np.linalg.norm`) before you take the dot product, and see what happens to the score when you stretch c1 again. Does the meter still get fooled?</cell id="cell-19">

cells.source: # How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you never heard of. Somewhere, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.

A song is not one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at once. Your taste is that same tangle. So the real question: how do you boil a match between two many-featured things down to a single number?

We're going to build one answer with code, cell by cell, and check it against itself as we go.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Think of a taste as an arrow. Each direction is a quality, and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Here's one amber probe (that's you) and four candidate songs, all as 2D arrows.

The rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add. That sum is the score.

Before you run this: the probe points straight along the x-axis. Which candidate do you think will score highest, and can any score go negative?

c1 tops out because the probe points its way, not because c1 is the longest arrow (c1, c2, and c3 are all the same length). And c3 already went negative.

Now turn the probe. Nothing about the candidates changes. Before you run this: predict which candidate takes over the top score once the probe points straight up instead.

The candidates never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the ranking flipped completely. So the score is not reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how well two directions agree.

Now the trap. Before you run this: stretch c1 to three times its length, but keep it pointing the exact same way. Does its score against the original probe change?

def cosine(a, b):
    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    return dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

c1_stretched = c1 * 3.0

score_before = dot(probe, c1)
score_after = dot(probe, c1_stretched)
print("score before stretch:", score_before)
print("score after stretch: ", score_after)

same_direction = np.isclose(cosine(probe, c1), cosine(probe, c1_stretched))
print("direction unchanged:", same_direction)

assert same_direction
assert score_after > score_before

c1 never turned. Its direction, measured by cosine, is identical before and after. But its score climbed anyway, purely because it got longer. Length cheats the meter. A big score does not mean "same song," it means "points the same way," and a long arrow can fake a high number.

## Name the machine you just used

Three probe positions, three readings. Point along a candidate, point across it, point against it. Before you run this: predict the sign of each score.

along = c1      # same direction as the probe
across = c2     # perpendicular to the probe
against = c3    # opposite direction from the probe

score_along = dot(probe, along)
score_across = dot(probe, across)
score_against = dot(probe, against)

print("along:  ", score_along)
print("across: ", score_across)
print("against:", score_against)

assert score_along > 0
assert score_across == 0
assert score_against < 0

Agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three readings.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in, one honest reading of alignment comes out. If that claim were false, the assert below would fail: aligned arrows would not score highest, perpendicular arrows would not land on exactly zero, and opposed arrows would not go negative.

readings = [score_along, score_across, score_against]
print("meter readings, agree -> ignore -> oppose:", readings)

assert readings[0] > readings[1] > readings[2]
assert readings[1] == 0.0

## Where the meter travels, and where it breaks

Same meter, different aisle. Swap songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what a role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply matched entries, add them up. The dot product never asked what the numbers meant.

# features: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]
you = np.array([8.0, 5.0, 2.0])
role = np.array([6.0, 4.0, 1.0])

fit_score = dot(you, role)
print("job fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == dot(role, you)

Same function, new domain, no code changed. That's exactly why the meter travels.

Now the honest look. Before you run this: if you double the role's requirements exactly, same direction, twice the numbers, does the fit score double too?

role_doubled = role * 2.0

fit_score_doubled = dot(you, role_doubled)
print("original fit score:", fit_score)
print("doubled-role score:", fit_score_doubled)

assert np.isclose(fit_score_doubled, 2.0 * fit_score)

It doubles exactly. The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling is the crack: a long, loud song can win on sheer size, and a padded resume can win on sheer length, because size passes straight through the meter untouched.

## For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

One thread to pull: try normalizing a vector by its own length (dividing by `np.linalg.norm`) before you take the dot product, and see what happens to the score when you stretch c1 again. Does the meter still get fooled?

+    "Before you run this: does the score double too, or does it just nudge up in some messy way?"
+    "print(\"Double one input, double the output. Size passes straight through the meter.\")"
+    "The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement.\n",
+    "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",

+    "Try coding it yourself: divide `probe` and each candidate by their own length (`np.linalg.norm`) before you call `multiply_and_add`. Rerun the stretched `c1` cell with the normalized vectors. Does stretching still change the score?"

tool_use_result.originalFile:
{
  "nbformat": 4,
  "nbformat_minor": 5,
  "metadata": {
    "kernelspec": {
      "display_name": "Python 3",
      "language": "python",
      "name": "python3"
    },
    "language_info": {
      "name": "python",
      "version": "3"
    }
  },
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you never heard of. Somewhere, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "A song is not one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at once. Your taste is that same tangle. So the real question: how do you boil a match between two many-featured things down to a single number?\n",
        "\n",
        "We're going to build one answer with code, cell by cell, and check it against itself as we go."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "\n",
        "def dot(a, b):\n",
        "    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=float)\n",
        "    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)\n",
        "    return np.sum(a * b)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
        "\n",
        "Think of a taste as an arrow. Each direction is a quality, and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Here's one amber probe (that's you) and four candidate songs, all as 2D arrows.\n",
        "\n",
        "The rule: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add. That sum is the score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: the probe points straight along the x-axis. Which candidate do you think will score highest, and can any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([1.0, 0.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "c1 = np.array([3.0, 0.0])\n",
        "c2 = np.array([0.0, 3.0])\n",
        "c3 = np.array([-3.0, 0.0])\n",
        "c4 = np.array([2.0, 2.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "candidates = {\"c1\": c1, \"c2\": c2, \"c3\": c3, \"c4\": c4}\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, \"->\", score)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "c1 tops out because the probe points its way, not because c1 is the longest arrow (c1, c2, and c3 are all the same length). And c3 already went negative.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now turn the probe. Nothing about the candidates changes. Before you run this: predict which candidate takes over the top score once the probe points straight up instead."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe_turned = np.array([0.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "scores_turned = {name: dot(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, score in scores_turned.items():\n",
        "    print(name, \"->\", score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert scores_turned[\"c2\"] > scores_turned[\"c1\"]\n",
        "assert scores[\"c1\"] > scores[\"c2\"]\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the ranking flipped completely. So the score is not reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how well two directions agree.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now the trap. Before you run this: stretch c1 to three times its length, but keep it pointing the exact same way. Does its score against the original probe change?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def cosine(a, b):\n",
        "    a = np.asarray(a, dtype=float)\n",
        "    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)\n",
        "    return dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))\n",
        "\n",
        "c1_stretched = c1 * 3.0\n",
        "\n",
        "score_before = dot(probe, c1)\n",
        "score_after = dot(probe, c1_stretched)\n",
        "print(\"score before stretch:\", score_before)\n",
        "print(\"score after stretch: \", score_after)\n",
        "\n",
        "same_direction = np.isclose(cosine(probe, c1), cosine(probe, c1_stretched))\n",
        "print(\"direction unchanged:\", same_direction)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert same_direction\n",
        "assert score_after > score_before\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "c1 never turned. Its direction, measured by cosine, is identical before and after. But its score climbed anyway, purely because it got longer. Length cheats the meter. A big score does not mean \"same song,\" it means \"points the same way,\" and a long arrow can fake a high number."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Three probe positions, three readings. Point along a candidate, point across it, point against it. Before you run this: predict the sign of each score."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "along = c1      # same direction as the probe\n",
        "across = c2     # perpendicular to the probe\n",
        "against = c3    # opposite direction from the probe\n",
        "\n",
        "score_along = dot(probe, along)\n",
        "score_across = dot(probe, across)\n",
        "score_against = dot(probe, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"along:  \", score_along)\n",
        "print(\"across: \", score_across)\n",
        "print(\"against:\", score_against)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert score_along > 0\n",
        "assert score_across == 0\n",
        "assert score_against < 0\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three readings.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Two lists of numbers go in, one honest reading of alignment comes out. If that claim were false, the assert below would fail: aligned arrows would not score highest, perpendicular arrows would not land on exactly zero, and opposed arrows would not go negative."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "readings = [score_along, score_across, score_against]\n",
        "print(\"meter readings, agree -> ignore -> oppose:\", readings)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert readings[0] > readings[1] > readings[2]\n",
        "assert readings[1] == 0.0\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Where the meter travels, and where it breaks\n",
        "\n",
        "Same meter, different aisle. Swap songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what a role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply matched entries, add them up. The dot product never asked what the numbers meant."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# features: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]\n",
        "you = np.array([8.0, 5.0, 2.0])\n",
        "role = np.array([6.0, 4.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score = dot(you, role)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit_score == dot(role, you)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same function, new domain, no code changed. That's exactly why the meter travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now the honest look. Before you run this: if you double the role's requirements exactly, same direction, twice the numbers, does the fit score double too?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "role_doubled = role * 2.0\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score_doubled = dot(you, role_doubled)\n",
        "print(\"original fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "print(\"doubled-role score:\", fit_score_doubled)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.isclose(fit_score_doubled, 2.0 * fit_score)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "It doubles exactly. The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling is the crack: a long, loud song can win on sheer size, and a padded resume can win on sheer length, because size passes straight through the meter untouched."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "One thread to pull: try normalizing a vector by its own length (dividing by `np.linalg.norm`) before you take the dot product, and see what happens to the score when you stretch c1 again. Does the meter still get fooled?"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Two descriptions got compared in a data center and the
      verdict was: same taste. But sit with what that comparison has to do. A
      song is not one number. It is loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at
      once, and your taste is that same tangle. So try it first. How would you
      boil the match between two many-featured things down to a single number?
      Harder than it sounds. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning."
    visual: "[visual: connect-to-reality] Two tall stacks of qualities slide in
      side by side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song, each row
      a named feature in periwinkle: loud, fast, sad, bright, and more trailing
      off the frame. A single lilac match readout waits with a question mark
      where its number should be. The question card holds over the unfilled
      readout, half the frame deliberately empty so the puzzle, not an answer,
      owns the screen."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: Collapsing two many-featured things into a single match number is a
      genuine puzzle, and it is what recommendation runs on.
    novelElements:
      - the two many-featured descriptions
      - the single match readout
    onScreenText:
      - How would you score the match?
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: false
    misconceptionBeat: false
    estimatedSeconds: 34
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: "You just tried to score that match by hand, and it fought back:
      too many qualities, no clean way to fold them into one number. Here is the
      move that lands it. A taste is an arrow: each direction is one quality,
      and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score, the whole tangle collapsed to one number. Before
      you touch the probe, call your shot: name the one candidate whose bar will
      top out. Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a
      candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score
      means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. Stretch one candidate
      longer without turning it at all, and its bar climbs anyway, on length
      alone, even though its direction never got closer. Length cheats the
      meter. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness, and a long
      arrow can fake a high number."
    visual: "[visual: covary | dynamic-concept] The stacked lists of qualities
      from the hook fold down, each feature becoming one direction, until each
      song is a single arrow. All arrows use thin exact strokes with arrowheads
      sized in proportion to shaft length; the amber probe's head is drawn to
      the same proportion as the candidates', never bloated or malformed. One
      amber probe arrow sweeps through directions; four periwinkle candidate
      arrows hold still; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a small
      mono numeric readout climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away
      from it. Each candidate label, and the 'probe' label, sits nudged clear of
      every arrow so nothing ever collides, staying readable at every probe
      angle including when the probe passes candidate B. Candidate c1 carries a
      length handle: dragging it stretches c1 while its direction holds, and
      c1's score climbs on length alone. A freeze frame before the sweep carries
      the prediction card that asks the learner to name the candidate whose bar
      will top out; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as
      integers on the bars."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: "Multiply pairwise and add: the score climbs with the probe's
      direction, so agreement is not identity, and stretching a candidate climbs
      the score on length alone, so size can fake it."
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
      - the candidate length handle
    onScreenText:
      - probe
      - candidates
      - 3  2  0  -2
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: "Before you touch the probe, call your shot: name the one candidate
        whose bar tops out."
      expectedWrongAnswer: The longest candidate, or the one that looks most
        similar, tops out, and no score can go negative because similarity
        cannot drop below zero.
    majorReveal: false
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 66
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: "Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero.
      Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it
      behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial. So say it
      plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go
      in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks
      direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed."
    visual: "[visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through
      ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence trues up on
      screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small lilac mono annotation,
      a . b, sits on the score readout."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: "The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative."
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - a . b
      - agree  ignore  oppose
      - The dot product is a similarity meter.
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      annotation: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    misconceptionBeat: false
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: Same meter, different aisle
    narration: "Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows
      go in, and which two operations turn them into a single score? While you
      think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score
      yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add
      them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The
      multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly
      why it travels."
    visual: '[visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The card holds the
      actual question, "Two arrows go in. What two operations turn them into the
      score?", while the meter idles. The song columns swap for two labeled
      "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free, six rows at
      full readable size with the whole frame to breathe; matched entries
      multiply and pool into the same lilac readout on the same dial, and "two
      arrows in, one number out" lands as the readout label. "Different aisle,
      same meter" lands as the closing line.'
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: The same multiply-and-add travels across domains untouched, because
      the recipe never asks what the numbers mean.
    novelElements:
      - the job-fit columns
    onScreenText:
      - Two arrows go in. What two operations turn them into the score?
      - two arrows in, one number out
      - different aisle, same meter
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: false
    misconceptionBeat: false
    estimatedSeconds: 30
  - id: beat-04b
    title: The honest look
    narration: "Now the honest look, and guess before you watch. Double c1's
      length exactly, leave its direction untouched, does the number double too?
      It does, exactly. Double one input, double the output: the meter is linear
      in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling is the crack. Long songs
      would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length,
      because size passes straight through. For the walk home: what is the
      cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size, now that you
      know size scales the score in lockstep? Still up? The menu below has three
      ways to keep going."
    visual: "[visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] A guess card holds
      with one candidate outlined and the score readout hidden; the card carries
      no header and instead poses the doubling question directly, asking whether
      doubling c1 doubles the number. Then that periwinkle candidate doubles in
      length while its direction holds still; its lilac score reads out at
      exactly twice its former value, the two numbers stacked with no header so
      the stacked 4.2 / x2 / 8.4 stands as the reveal itself; a small rose flag
      marks that size passed straight through. The walk-home question lands as
      the closing card under a single 'For the walk home' header, and a quiet
      chip points to the extensions menu on the page."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: The meter is linear in each input, so doubling one arrow exactly
      doubles the score, and that clean scaling is the crack the cheapest fix
      will exploit.
    novelElements:
      - the doubled arrow
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - Double c1. Same direction. Does the score double?
      - For the walk home
      - Keep the direction. Forget the size.
      - Still up?
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: If you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long,
        does the number double too?
      expectedWrongAnswer: "No: stretching one arrow nudges the number up in some
        messy way, not a clean doubling."
    majorReveal: true
    misconceptionBeat: false
    estimatedSeconds: 40

file.content:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

Here's the part worth chewing on before you nod along. A song isn't one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, a dozen qualities at once, and your taste is that same tangle. So before any machine gets a turn, take your own swing at it. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Try answering it first. It's genuinely harder than it sounds. You've got two stacks of features and you want one number for how well they fit. Add the stacks? That rewards loudness, not agreement. Line them up and count exact matches? Real features aren't yes or no. The moment there are more than a couple of qualities, the honest score stops being obvious.

One answer to this question runs half of modern machine learning. The puzzle was never the machine. The puzzle is you, right now, trying to fold a tangle into a single fair number.

Take a guess before the next section gives it away: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into one match score, what would you actually do to them? Write it down. A guess you can be wrong about beats ten definitions you nod along to.

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

You just tried to score that match by hand, and it fought back: too many qualities, no clean way to fold them into one number. Here's the move that lands it.

Strip a taste down until it fits in your palm. It's an arrow. Each direction is one quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing. Take one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add, and the whole tangle collapses to one number.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so the reading stays clean while the machine does the same work silently.

Call your shot before anything moves: name the one candidate whose bar tops out. Say it out loud. The tempting guess is that the arrow that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction did. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction. And here's the sneaky part: stretch one candidate longer without turning it, and its bar climbs anyway, on length alone, even though its direction never got closer. Length cheats the meter. A long arrow can fake a high number.

The number measures which way two arrows point. It also measures how big they are. Those are two different jobs sharing one readout.

Before you scroll on, call one reading you haven't seen yet: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out. And it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, where $a$ is the probe's list and $b$ is the candidate's, the dot standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add. There are just more entries. The dial in your head stays as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

While every arrow held the same length, direction was all the sum ever responded to. That's why the meter tracks alignment. It answers the question you actually moved.

The meter has three readings worth knowing: positive, zero, negative. Say what each one claims about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words, before you go on.

## Same meter, different aisle

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

> Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

Now keep the same meter running and walk it one aisle over. We don't rebuild anything. It's the same meter, just pointing its needle at a job posting instead of a song. The role wants Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. In the job aisle, which list plays the probe? It's you, the one asking the question. The role is the candidate you're scoring. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. First point the probe straight along c1 and set its length back to 1.0, so it reads a clean 1.0. Then guess before you read on: if you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

It does, exactly. Double one input, double the output. The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it: 4.2 becomes 8.4, no surprise hiding in the middle. That clean scaling is the crack. Size passes straight through the multiply-and-add, untouched.

That's the problem. A long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

Remember where we started: two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was same taste. Now you can run that comparison yourself. Next time the app hands you a stranger's song that is exactly right, you will know the number that said ship it, and you will know that number can be shouted over.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size, now that you know size scales the score in lockstep?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

input.content:
{
 "nbformat": 4,
 "nbformat_minor": 5,
 "metadata": {
  "kernelspec": {
   "display_name": "Python 3",
   "language": "python",
   "name": "python3"
  },
  "language_info": {
   "name": "python",
   "version": "3.x"
  }
 },
 "cells": [
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## The score behind the song\n",
    "\n",
    "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.\n",
    "\n",
    "A song isn't one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, a bunch of qualities at once, and your taste is that same tangle. So before we build anything, try the puzzle yourself.\n",
    "\n",
    "Below are two lists of numbers: your `taste` across four qualities, and a `song_loud` that's simply big across every quality. There's also a `song_match` that's actually close to your taste, quality by quality.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: if you score a song by just adding up every number in both lists, which one do you think wins, the song that's actually close to your taste, or the one that's just loud everywhere?\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "import numpy as np\n",
    "\n",
    "features = [\"loud\", \"fast\", \"sad\", \"bright\"]\n",
    "taste = np.array([9, 2, 1, 8])\n",
    "song_match = np.array([8, 3, 2, 7])\n",
    "song_loud = np.array([9, 9, 9, 9])\n",
    "\n",
    "naive_score_match = taste.sum() + song_match.sum()\n",
    "naive_score_loud = taste.sum() + song_loud.sum()\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"naive score, actual match:\", round(float(naive_score_match), 2))\n",
    "print(\"naive score, just loud:  \", round(float(naive_score_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "true_gap_match = np.abs(taste - song_match).sum()\n",
    "true_gap_loud = np.abs(taste - song_loud).sum()\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, actual match:\", round(float(true_gap_match), 2))\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, just loud:  \", round(float(true_gap_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert naive_score_loud > naive_score_match\n",
    "assert true_gap_match < true_gap_loud"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Just adding the lists together gets fooled. The loud song wins on the naive score even though the quality-by-quality gap shows it's actually the worse match. Sheer size can fake a good score. Try answering the puzzle yourself before you read on: what would you do instead? One answer to this runs half of modern machine learning.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
    "\n",
    "Here's the move that lands it. Picture each list as an arrow: each direction is one quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs), placed at these angles from north:\n",
    "\n",
    "- `probe`: 40 degrees\n",
    "- `c1`: 75 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c2`: 40 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c3`: 130 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c4`: 220 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "\n",
    "The score is: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: name the one candidate whose score tops out, given those angles.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def vec(angle_deg, length=1.0):\n",
    "    a = np.deg2rad(angle_deg)\n",
    "    return length * np.array([np.cos(a), np.sin(a)])\n",
    "\n",
    "probe = vec(40)\n",
    "c1 = vec(75, 1.0)\n",
    "c2 = vec(40, 1.0)\n",
    "c3 = vec(130, 1.0)\n",
    "c4 = vec(220, 1.0)\n",
    "\n",
    "candidates = {\"c1\": c1, \"c2\": c2, \"c3\": c3, \"c4\": c4}\n",
    "scores = {name: float(np.sum(probe * arrow)) for name, arrow in candidates.items()}\n",
    "\n",
    "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
    "    print(name, \"score:\", round(score, 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "top_candidate = max(scores, key=scores.get)\n",
    "print(\"tops out:\", top_candidate)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert top_candidate == \"c2\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`c2` shares the probe's exact direction, so it tops out. Multiply entry by entry, add up the products: that's the whole mechanism. This is called the dot product, and NumPy has a shortcut for it, `np.dot`, which we'll check against the manual version shortly.\n",
    "\n",
    "But watch what happens to a score when nothing about direction changes at all. Before you run this: if you stretch `c1` to 1.3 times its length, without turning it at all, do you think it can overtake `c2`, even though `c2` still points more exactly at the probe?\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "c1_before = float(np.sum(probe * c1))\n",
    "c1_stretched = c1 * 1.3\n",
    "c1_after = float(np.sum(probe * c1_stretched))\n",
    "c2_score = float(np.sum(probe * c2))\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"c1 score before stretch:\", round(c1_before, 2))\n",
    "print(\"c1 score after stretch: \", round(c1_after, 2))\n",
    "print(\"c2 score (unchanged):   \", round(c2_score, 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert c1_before < c2_score\n",
    "assert c1_after > c2_score"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`c1` never turned an inch, but stretching it let it overtake `c2`, the candidate that actually points at the probe. Length cheats the meter. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness, and a long arrow can fake a high number.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Name the machine you just used\n",
    "\n",
    "Point the probe along a candidate and the score climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the score dies to zero. Point it against and the score goes negative.\n",
    "\n",

    "against = vec(220, 1.0)   # 180 degrees off the probe\n",
    "\n",
    "score_along = float(np.sum(probe * along))\n",
    "score_across = float(np.sum(probe * across))\n",
    "score_against = float(np.sum(probe * against))\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"along:  \", round(score_along, 2))\n",
    "print(\"across: \", round(score_across, 2))\n",
    "print(\"against:\", round(score_against, 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert score_along > 0\n",
    "assert np.isclose(score_across, 0.0, atol=1e-9)\n",
    "assert score_against < 0"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle.\n",
    "\n",
    "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
    "\n",
    "Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out. We write it $a \\cdot b$: multiply pairwise, then add. Let's check that our manual version and NumPy's shortcut agree, because that's the whole mechanism, nothing hidden inside it.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "pairs = [(probe, along), (probe, across), (probe, against), (probe, c1_stretched)]\n",
    "\n",
    "for a, b in pairs:\n",
    "    manual = np.sum(a * b)\n",
    "    shortcut = np.dot(a, b)\n",
    "    assert np.isclose(manual, shortcut)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"manual multiply-and-add matches np.dot every time.\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Same meter, different aisle\n",
    "\n",
    "Rebuild the meter from memory before you read on: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into a score?\n",
    "\n",
    "Now walk that same meter one aisle over. Swap songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: given `you = [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]` and `role` wants similar hours and reps but fewer nights free, do you expect the fit score to be a fairly large positive number, close to zero, or negative?\n"
   ]
  },

   "cell_type": "code",

   "execution_count": null,

   ]

   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "a = probe\n",
    "b = c1\n",
    "\n",
    "score_before = float(np.dot(a, b))\n",
    "b_doubled = b * 2\n",
    "score_after = float(np.dot(a, b_doubled))\n",
    "\n",

   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Double one input, double the output, exactly. The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling is the crack: a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. Size passes straight through the multiply-and-add, untouched.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## For the walk home\n",
    "\n",
    "Next time your music app hands you a stranger's song that's exactly right, you'll know the number underneath that said ship it, and you'll know that number can be shouted over.\n",
    "\n",

    "\n",

 ]

<cell id="cell-0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
</cell id="cell-0">
<cell id="cell-1"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.

A song is not one number. It is loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at once. Your taste is that same tangle. So before we build anything: how would you boil the match between two many-featured things down to a single number?

We're going to build one answer with our hands, cell by cell.</cell id="cell-1">
<cell id="cell-2"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>A taste is an arrow: each entry is one quality. Below, `probe` is you, and each candidate is a song.

Before you run this: just look at the numbers. Which candidate do you guess will end up scoring highest against the probe?</cell id="cell-2">
<cell id="cell-3">import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A taste is an arrow: each entry is one quality (say, "loud" and "bright").
probe = np.array([1.0, 0.0])

candidates = {
    "c1": np.array([3.0, 5.0]),
    "c2": np.array([2.0, -1.0]),
    "c3": np.array([0.0, 4.0]),
    "c4": np.array([-2.0, 3.0]),
}

print("probe:", probe)
for name, vec in candidates.items():
    print(name, vec)</cell id="cell-3">
<cell id="cell-4"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.

Before you run this: does any score come out negative? Say a guess out loud.</cell id="cell-4">
<cell id="cell-5">def multiply_and_add(a, b):
    """Multiply entry by entry, then add. That's the whole recipe."""
    return np.sum(a * b)

scores = {name: multiply_and_add(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}
print(scores)

for name, vec in candidates.items():
    assert multiply_and_add(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)

print("c1 has the top score:", scores["c1"] == max(scores.values()))</cell id="cell-5">
<cell id="cell-6"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Now turn the probe. It sweeps through directions while a candidate holds still.

Before you run this: as the probe turns away from a candidate, do you expect its score to fall smoothly, or to jump straight to zero and stay there?</cell id="cell-6">
<cell id="cell-7">def rotate(v, angle_rad):
    c, s = np.cos(angle_rad), np.sin(angle_rad)
    rotation = np.array([[c, -s], [s, c]])
    return rotation @ v

target = candidates["c1"]

print(f"{'angle (deg)':>12} | score")
for angle_deg in [0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180]:
    probe_now = rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(angle_deg))
    score = multiply_and_add(probe_now, target)
    print(f"{angle_deg:>12} | {score:6.2f}")

score_at_0 = multiply_and_add(rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(0)), target)
score_at_180 = multiply_and_add(rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(180)), target)
assert score_at_0 > 0
assert score_at_180 < 0
print("Turn toward it and the score climbs. Turn away and it dies, then goes negative.")</cell id="cell-7">
<cell id="cell-8"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch one more thing before you decide.

Before you run this: stretch a candidate's length, but keep pointing it the exact same direction. Does its score climb, even though nothing about its direction changed?</cell id="cell-8">
<cell id="cell-9">c1 = candidates["c1"]

c1_original_score = multiply_and_add(probe, c1)
c1_stretched = c1 * 2.0
c1_stretched_score = multiply_and_add(probe, c1_stretched)

print("original score:", c1_original_score)
print("stretched score:", c1_stretched_score)

def cosine(a, b):
    return multiply_and_add(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

# Direction never changed. Cosine similarity (angle-only) proves it.
assert np.isclose(cosine(probe, c1), cosine(probe, c1_stretched))
assert c1_stretched_score > c1_original_score
print("Direction held steady, but the score still climbed. Length cheats the meter.")</cell id="cell-9">
<cell id="cell-10"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and it dies to zero. Point it against and it goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. We write it `a . b`: multiply pairwise, then add.

Before you run this: guess the sign of each of the three readings below, agree, ignore, oppose, before you see the numbers.</cell id="cell-10">
<cell id="cell-11">reference = np.array([3.0, 0.0])
along = np.array([2.0, 0.0])
across = np.array([0.0, 2.0])
against = np.array([-2.0, 0.0])

agree = multiply_and_add(reference, along)
ignore = multiply_and_add(reference, across)
oppose = multiply_and_add(reference, against)

print("agree:", agree, " ignore:", ignore, " oppose:", oppose)

assert agree > 0
assert ignore == 0
assert oppose < 0
print("The dot product is a similarity meter: agree, ignore, oppose.")</cell id="cell-11">
<cell id="cell-12"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Multiply pairwise, then add. If that came back without a peek, `a . b` is yours now.

While that settles, walk the same meter one aisle over. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants.

Before you run this: do you need to change the function at all to score a job fit instead of a song match?</cell id="cell-12">
<cell id="cell-13"># Same meter, different aisle: score yourself against a job posting.
you = np.array([8.0, 5.0, 3.0])   # python hours, sql reps, nights free
role = np.array([6.0, 4.0, 0.0])  # what the role wants

fit_score = multiply_and_add(you, role)
print("fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role)
print("Same recipe, new domain. The dot product never asked what the numbers meant.")</cell id="cell-13">
<cell id="cell-14"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Now the honest look. Double `c1`'s length exactly, leave its direction untouched.

Before you run this: does the score double too, or does it just nudge up in some messy way?</cell id="cell-14">
<cell id="cell-15">c1_double = c1 * 2.0
score_c1 = multiply_and_add(probe, c1)
score_c1_double = multiply_and_add(probe, c1_double)

print("score:", score_c1, " doubled input score:", score_c1_double)

assert np.isclose(score_c1_double, 2 * score_c1)
print("Double one input, double the output. Size passes straight through the meter.")</cell id="cell-15">
<cell id="cell-16"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## For the walk home

The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement.

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try coding it yourself: divide `probe` and each candidate by their own length (`np.linalg.norm`) before you call `multiply_and_add`. Rerun the stretched `c1` cell with the normalized vectors. Does stretching still change the score?</cell id="cell-16">

cells.source: # How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.

A song is not one number. It is loud, fast, sad, bright, many qualities at once. Your taste is that same tangle. So before we build anything: how would you boil the match between two many-featured things down to a single number?

We're going to build one answer with our hands, cell by cell.

A taste is an arrow: each entry is one quality. Below, `probe` is you, and each candidate is a song.

Before you run this: just look at the numbers. Which candidate do you guess will end up scoring highest against the probe?

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A taste is an arrow: each entry is one quality (say, "loud" and "bright").
probe = np.array([1.0, 0.0])

candidates = {
    "c1": np.array([3.0, 5.0]),
    "c2": np.array([2.0, -1.0]),
    "c3": np.array([0.0, 4.0]),
    "c4": np.array([-2.0, 3.0]),
}

print("probe:", probe)
for name, vec in candidates.items():
    print(name, vec)

Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add.

Before you run this: does any score come out negative? Say a guess out loud.

Now turn the probe. It sweeps through directions while a candidate holds still.

Before you run this: as the probe turns away from a candidate, do you expect its score to fall smoothly, or to jump straight to zero and stay there?

def rotate(v, angle_rad):
    c, s = np.cos(angle_rad), np.sin(angle_rad)
    rotation = np.array([[c, -s], [s, c]])
    return rotation @ v

target = candidates["c1"]

print(f"{'angle (deg)':>12} | score")
for angle_deg in [0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180]:
    probe_now = rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(angle_deg))
    score = multiply_and_add(probe_now, target)
    print(f"{angle_deg:>12} | {score:6.2f}")

score_at_0 = multiply_and_add(rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(0)), target)
score_at_180 = multiply_and_add(rotate(np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.deg2rad(180)), target)
assert score_at_0 > 0
assert score_at_180 < 0
print("Turn toward it and the score climbs. Turn away and it dies, then goes negative.")

So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch one more thing before you decide.

Before you run this: stretch a candidate's length, but keep pointing it the exact same direction. Does its score climb, even though nothing about its direction changed?

c1 = candidates["c1"]

c1_original_score = multiply_and_add(probe, c1)
c1_stretched = c1 * 2.0
c1_stretched_score = multiply_and_add(probe, c1_stretched)

print("original score:", c1_original_score)
print("stretched score:", c1_stretched_score)

def cosine(a, b):
    return multiply_and_add(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

# Direction never changed. Cosine similarity (angle-only) proves it.
assert np.isclose(cosine(probe, c1), cosine(probe, c1_stretched))
assert c1_stretched_score > c1_original_score
print("Direction held steady, but the score still climbed. Length cheats the meter.")

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and it dies to zero. Point it against and it goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. We write it `a . b`: multiply pairwise, then add.

Before you run this: guess the sign of each of the three readings below, agree, ignore, oppose, before you see the numbers.

reference = np.array([3.0, 0.0])
along = np.array([2.0, 0.0])
across = np.array([0.0, 2.0])
against = np.array([-2.0, 0.0])

agree = multiply_and_add(reference, along)
ignore = multiply_and_add(reference, across)
oppose = multiply_and_add(reference, against)

print("agree:", agree, " ignore:", ignore, " oppose:", oppose)

assert agree > 0
assert ignore == 0
assert oppose < 0
print("The dot product is a similarity meter: agree, ignore, oppose.")

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score? Multiply pairwise, then add. If that came back without a peek, `a . b` is yours now.

While that settles, walk the same meter one aisle over. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants.

Before you run this: do you need to change the function at all to score a job fit instead of a song match?

# Same meter, different aisle: score yourself against a job posting.
you = np.array([8.0, 5.0, 3.0])   # python hours, sql reps, nights free
role = np.array([6.0, 4.0, 0.0])  # what the role wants

fit_score = multiply_and_add(you, role)
print("fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role)
print("Same recipe, new domain. The dot product never asked what the numbers meant.")

Now the honest look. Double `c1`'s length exactly, leave its direction untouched.

Before you run this: does the score double too, or does it just nudge up in some messy way?

c1_double = c1 * 2.0
score_c1 = multiply_and_add(probe, c1)
score_c1_double = multiply_and_add(probe, c1_double)

print("score:", score_c1, " doubled input score:", score_c1_double)

assert np.isclose(score_c1_double, 2 * score_c1)
print("Double one input, double the output. Size passes straight through the meter.")

## For the walk home

The meter is linear in each arrow you feed it. That clean scaling means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement.

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try coding it yourself: divide `probe` and each candidate by their own length (`np.linalg.norm`) before you call `multiply_and_add`. Rerun the stretched `c1` cell with the normalized vectors. Does stretching still change the score?

{
 "nbformat": 4,
 "nbformat_minor": 5,
 "metadata": {
  "kernelspec": {
   "display_name": "Python 3",
   "language": "python",
   "name": "python3"
  },
  "language_info": {
   "name": "python",
   "version": "3.x"
  }
 },
 "cells": [
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## The score behind the song\n",
    "\n",
    "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the verdict was: same taste.\n",
    "\n",
    "A song isn't one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, a bunch of qualities at once, and your taste is that same tangle. So before we build anything, try the puzzle yourself.\n",
    "\n",
    "Below are two lists of numbers: your `taste` across four qualities, and a `song_loud` that's simply big across every quality. There's also a `song_match` that's actually close to your taste, quality by quality.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: if you score a song by just adding up every number in both lists, which one do you think wins, the song that's actually close to your taste, or the one that's just loud everywhere?\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "import numpy as np\n",
    "\n",
    "features = [\"loud\", \"fast\", \"sad\", \"bright\"]\n",
    "taste = np.array([9, 2, 1, 8])\n",
    "song_match = np.array([8, 3, 2, 7])\n",
    "song_loud = np.array([9, 9, 9, 9])\n",
    "\n",
    "naive_score_match = taste.sum() + song_match.sum()\n",
    "naive_score_loud = taste.sum() + song_loud.sum()\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"naive score, actual match:\", round(float(naive_score_match), 2))\n",
    "print(\"naive score, just loud:  \", round(float(naive_score_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "true_gap_match = np.abs(taste - song_match).sum()\n",
    "true_gap_loud = np.abs(taste - song_loud).sum()\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, actual match:\", round(float(true_gap_match), 2))\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, just loud:  \", round(float(true_gap_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert naive_score_loud > naive_score_match\n",
    "assert true_gap_match < true_gap_loud"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Just adding the lists together gets fooled. The loud song wins on the naive score even though the quality-by-quality gap shows it's actually the worse match. Sheer size can fake a good score. Try answering the puzzle yourself before you read on: what would you do instead? One answer to this runs half of modern machine learning.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
    "\n",
    "Here's the move that lands it. Picture each list as an arrow: each direction is one quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs), placed at these angles from north:\n",
    "\n",
    "- `probe`: 40 degrees\n",
    "- `c1`: 75 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c2`: 40 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c3`: 130 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "- `c4`: 220 degrees, length 1.0\n",
    "\n",
    "The score is: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products.\n",
    "\n",
    "Before you run this: name the one candidate whose score tops out, given those angles.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},
   "execution_count": null,
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def vec(angle_deg, length=1.0):\n",
    "    a = np.deg2rad(angle_deg)\n",
    "    return length * np.array([np.cos(a), np.sin(a)])\n",
    "\n",
    "probe = vec(40)\n",
    "c1 = vec(75, 1.0)\n",
    "c2 = vec(40, 1.0)\n",
    "c3 = vec(130, 1.0)\n",
    "c4 = vec(220, 1.0)\n",
    "\n",
    "candidates = {\"c1\": c1, \"c2\": c2, \"c3\": c3, \"c4\": c4}\n",
    "scores = {name: float(np.sum(probe * arrow)) for name, arrow in candidates.items()}\n",
    "\n",
    "for name, score in scores.items():\n",
    "    print(name, \"score:\", round(score, 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "top_candidate = max(scores, key=scores.get)\n",
    "print(\"tops out:\", top_candidate)\n",
    "\n",
    "assert top_candidate == \"c2\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "`c2` shares the probe's exact direction, so it tops out. Multiply entry by entry, add up the products: that's the whole mechanism. This is called the dot product, and NumPy has a shortcut for it, `np.dot`, which we'll check against the manual version shortly.\n",
    "\n",

   "cell_type": "code",
   "metadata": {},

   "outputs": [],

    "\n",

    "song_loud = np.array([9, 9, 9, 9])\n",
    "\n",
    "naive_score_match = taste.sum() + song_match.sum()\n",
    "naive_score_loud = taste.sum() + song_loud.sum()\n",
    "\n",

    "print(\"naive score, just loud:  \", round(float(naive_score_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "true_gap_match = np.abs(taste - song_match).sum()\n",
    "true_gap_loud = np.abs(taste - song_loud).sum()\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, actual match:\", round(float(true_gap_match), 2))\n",
    "print(\"quality-by-quality gap, just loud:  \", round(float(true_gap_loud), 2))\n",
    "\n",
    "assert naive_score_loud > naive_score_match\n",
    "assert true_gap_match < true_gap_loud"
